channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Live Nation and Ticketmaster Illegally Monopolized Ticketing Market, Jury Finds
item.description: In a verdict that could have far-reaching consequences in the music industry, the live colossus that includes Ticketmaster was found to have violated antitrust laws.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: Live Nation faced allegations of antitrust violations in court even though the federal government settled with the company soon after the proceedings began.

item.title: Oscar Isaac and Carey Mulligan Have a Generational ‘Beef’
item.description: The actors play a married couple on the brink in the second season of the Netflix anthology series.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer and Sela Shiloni
item.media:credit: Sela Shiloni for The New York Times

item.title: As Los Angeles Rebuilds, a Surge of Experimentation
item.description: Across the city’s fire zones, there’s a surge of experimentation — collective rebuilding, catalog homes and new technologies that are safe and reduce costs.
item.dc:creator: Sam Lubell and Carlos Jaramillo
item.media:description: An empty home lot overlooking the Pacific after the fires, one of 16,000 homes destroyed. Challenges to rebuilding are stiff: labor shortages, tariff-induced cost spikes, lower-than-expected insurance payouts, and slow-moving bureaucracies. Cue the innovators.

item.title: They Were YouTube’s First Stars. Here’s What They Wish They’d Known.
item.description: MatPat, Miranda Sings, Grace Helbig and WheezyWaiter hit it big on YouTube long before it became a behemoth. They have thoughts about what it takes to succeed there.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: In Kannywood, a Film Scene Thrives Despite Censorship
item.description: A city in Northern Nigeria has turned into a moviemaking machine, churning out hundreds of productions a year.
item.dc:creator: Ricci Shryock
item.media:credit: Ricci Shryock for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ibrahim Bala (left) directs a scene in his Kannywood film “The Footprint of the Elephant.” Bala has been a filmmaker in Kano for 12 years and has directed more than 30 films.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From the Live Nation Antitrust Trial
item.description: A jury found that the concert giant operated as a monopoly, a verdict that could have major reverberations in the music industry.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: A judge will now determine damages and remedies in the case, which could involve divestments or a breakup of the company.

item.title: Review: ‘Margo’s Got Money Troubles’ Will Make an OnlyFan of You
item.description: Elle Fanning stars in a big-hearted, open-minded dramedy about online exposure and its complications.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik

item.title: ‘Giant’ Revisits Roald Dahl’s Antisemitic Comments: What to Know
item.description: Mark Rosenblatt’s Broadway play, starring John Lithgow as the British children’s book author, draws from Dahl’s comments over the years.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Ronald Dumont/Daily Express, via Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The British author Roald Dahl in 1971.

item.title: Paris Man Wins $1.2 Million Picasso Painting in Charity Raffle
item.description: A Parisian software salesman entered a charity raffle and came away with a piece of history: “I have some paintings, but not like a Picasso.”
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor

item.title: Billy Crystal to Return to Broadway With Solo Show ‘860’ About Losing His Home in L.A. Fires
item.description: The actor-comedian said he will return to Broadway this fall with a new solo show called “860,” named for the address of his destroyed family home.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Kevin Winter/Getty Images
item.media:description: Billy Crystal at this year’s Academy Awards ceremony in March.

item.title: ‘The Adding Machine’ Review: A Man Is Made Redundant
item.description: Daphne Rubin-Vega stars as a laid-off office worker who spins into a murderous rage in this update of Elmer L. Rice’s 1923 classic.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Daphne Rubin-Vega as Mr. Zero, both physically and figuratively a little man, in “The Adding Machine” at the Theater at St. Clement’s in Manhattan.

item.title: Review: Here’s Johnny! (And Bach and 4 Choreographers)
item.description: The violinist Johnny Gandelsman wanted his music to move. In the overly winsome “Johnny Loves Johann,” he performs Bach’s cello suites alongside four dance artists.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas

item.title: Julio Torres, Jennifer Tilly and More Star in Plays That Are Streaming Now
item.description: Other picks include the historical hip-hop musical “Mexodus,” an Anne Carson radio play and a century-old play about machines replacing humans.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.category: The Most Spectacularly Lamentable Trial of Miz Martha Washington (Play)
item.media:credit: Emilio Madrid
item.media:description: Julio Torres in his first Off Broadway play, “Color Theories,” which can now be streamed on HBO.

item.title: Daring and Dazzling, a New LACMA Floats Above Los Angeles
item.description: After $724 million and a decade of battles, the pugnacious David Geffen Galleries reassert the city’s role as a petri dish for experimental design.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kimmelman and Jake Michaels
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art

item.title: Stephen Colbert Wants the Vice President to Lay Off the Pope
item.description: The “Late Show” host scolded JD Vance for suggesting that Pope Leo XIV “be careful when he talks about matters of theology.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Review: ‘The Fear of 13,’ With Adrien Brody and Tessa Thompson, Doesn’t Add Up
item.description: Adrien Brody and Tessa Thompson make confident Broadway debuts, but the uneven script makes for a narratively slippery prison drama.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Adrien Brody, left, and Ephraim Sykes in the Lindsey Ferrentino play “The Fear of 13” at the James Earl Jones Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Barbara Gordon, 90, Dies; Wrote a Best Seller About Her Pill Addiction
item.description: Her 1979 memoir, “I’m Dancing as Fast as I Can,” which also became a movie, detailed years of prescription drug abuse and offered an indictment of American psychiatry.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: Im Dancing as Fast as I Can (Book)

item.title: The Good List: 7 Things to Add Some Delight to Your Day
item.description: Train jazz, the “Brady Bunch” house and the gift of time.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: The Aldrich Contemporary Art Museum Puts Connecticut Artists in the Spotlight
item.description: The Aldrich Contemporary Art Museum in Ridgefield will showcase works by people who live and work in New York’s shadow.
item.dc:creator: Annabel Keenan
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lula Mae Blocton of Eastern Connecticut State University works on a painting in her home studio.

item.title: In the Tech Heart of Texas, an Art Show Built on Data, Code and A.I.
item.description: The showcase features works that change from hour to hour, invite interaction and interrogate the idea of creativity itself.
item.dc:creator: Jillian Anthony
item.category: Blanton, Jack S, Museum of Art

item.title: Honoring Frederic Church: Beyond the Hudson River School
item.description: His many achievements have been obscured, some believe, by his reputation as a provincial landscape painter.
item.dc:creator: Hilarie M. Sheets
item.media:credit: via The Olana Partnership
item.media:description: “Our Banner in the Sky” (1861) will be included in the exhibition “Frederic Church: Global Artist,” opening May 17 at the Olana State Historic Site.

item.title: ‘Rethinking, Reimagining and Reinstalling’ the Metropolitan Museum of Art
item.description: A $1.5 billion project will transform the nation’s most-visited art museum, with renovations involving a quarter of the galleries and public spaces.
item.dc:creator: John Hanc
item.media:credit: Daniel Terna for The New York Times

item.title: What to See This Spring at Museums Across the U.S.
item.description: The season includes a Duchamp retrospective at MoMA, a window on Etruscan civilization at the de Young in San Francisco and a fashion celebration at the Phoenix Art Museum.
item.dc:creator: Morgan Malget
item.category: de Young, M H, Memorial Museum
New Britain Museum of American Art
Museum of Fine Arts, St Petersburg (Fla)

item.title: Elizabeth Banks Reflects on 33 Years of Marriage
item.description: The “Miniature Wife” star on why she and her husband have chosen each other, over and over again.
item.dc:creator: Anna Martin, Elisa Gutierrez, Emily Lang, Lynn Levy, Daniel Ramirez and Dan Powell
item.media:credit: Maggie Shannon for The New York Times
item.media:description: The actress Elizabeth Banks.

item.title: ‘No Picnic’ Is a Walk Down Mean Street Memory Lane
item.description: This Philip Hartman movie, shot in the East Village in 1985 and now restored, shows at Film Forum through April 23.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman

item.title: Obsessed With the Titanic? These Historical Fictional Books Will Transport You.
item.description: This gripping historical fiction will transport you to the doomed ship and back to land.
item.dc:creator: Donna Jones Alward

item.title: Late Night Laughs at Explanations of Trump’s Jesus ‘Joke’
item.description: Ronny Chieng poked fun at JD Vance’s defense of President Trump posting an image of himself as a Christlike figure.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Taylor Frankie Paul Will Not Face New Domestic Violence Charges
item.description: Prosecutors in Utah investigated after the reality star’s ex-boyfriend told the police she had scratched, shoved and struck him during a fight in February.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives (TV Program)

item.title: The Museums That Helped Power Atlanta’s Rise Are Still Pushing Ahead
item.description: For 100 years, the Atlanta History Center and the High Museum of Art have expanded and diversified, not unlike the metropolis itself.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Kendrick Brinson for The New York Times

item.title: A New Exhibition at New York’s Natural History Museum Honors Fossil Hunters
item.description: In a new and ongoing exhibition, the American Museum of Natural History highlights the findings of Mark Norell and other fossil hunters responsible for its most important discoveries.
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Stein
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paleontologists and their team excavate an Ankylosaur, an armored dinosaur, at Camel’s Humps in the Gobi Desert in Mongolia in 2005.

item.title: Moon Rocks! 9 Lunar Songs for the Artemis Crew
item.description: Hear from David Bowie, Neko Case, Arthur Russell and more artists inspired by our poetic, mysterious satellite.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Michael Ochs Archives/Getty Images
item.media:description: David Bowie, one of rock’s premier aliens.

item.title: At a Difficult Time, a Minnesota Museum Offers Respite to Somalis
item.description: As one of the few institutions of its kind in the world, the Somali Museum of Minnesota has become a center of the immigrant community.
item.dc:creator: Alex V. Cipolle
item.media:credit: Jenn Ackerman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The interior of a Somali hut in the Somali Museum of Minnesota. The dome is woven in an intricate diamond pattern from vibrant yarns, acacia tree fibers and dried grasses.

item.title: The Benefactor of the Pulitzer Arts Foundation Curates Its Collection
item.description: Emily Rauh Pulitzer, a veteran curator and collector, leans heavily on sculpture and drawing in a show of some 85 works.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Neeta Satam for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Planet Money’ Is Now a Book. And a Game. And a Record Label.
item.description: The long-running economics show on NPR is mining whimsical product experiments for content (and revenue) in a financially challenging environment.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.category: Planet Money: A Guide to the Economic Forces That Shape Your Life (Book)

item.title: Can ‘Michael’ Help Restore Jackson’s Image? His Estate Is Banking on It.
item.description: A new biopic is the latest move in the Jackson estate’s posthumous — and lucrative — rehabilitation campaign.
item.dc:creator: Mark Binelli

item.title: What Does an American Orchestra Need in a Conductor?
item.description: At a challenging time for American orchestras, the question of what a community wants from a music director has become more urgent.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Brendan Bannon for The New York Times
item.media:description: JoAnn Falletta, the longtime conductor of the Buffalo Philharmonic Orchestra, said she has come to believe that an orchestra leader needs to be a visible presence both at the podium and in the community.

item.title: Sofia Isella’s Dark Pop Is Poetic, Feminist and Right on Time
item.description: The 21-year-old singer, songwriter and producer often cakes herself in dirt — a representation of the cultural filth she sings and chants about. Her new EP is out Friday.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Horn and Chantal Anderson
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times

item.title: Route 66, a ‘Linear Museum Stretched Across Eight States,’ Turns 100
item.description: Institutions large and small examine the complicated history of the iconic corridor that helped define the American road trip.
item.dc:creator: Janelle Conaway
item.category: Albuquerque Museum of Art and History
item.media:credit: Anna Mardo/Getty Images
item.media:description: Although Route 66 is no longer an official U.S. highway, the road has gained traction in the popular imagination and has become a draw for national and international tourism.

item.title: Why Does the “Rocky” Statue Draw Crowds? This Show Investigates.
item.description: This spring, the Philadelphia Museum of Art invites the bronze boxer inside to center an exhibition on why we make monuments and what they mean.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.category: Monument Lab (Philadelphia, Pa, Public Art Studio)

item.title: San Francisco’s Modern Art Museum Reimagines the Fisher Collection
item.description: The Bay Area family made a deal with SFMOMA that called for exhibitions of the collection’s works every 10 years. Some 250 pieces are now showing.
item.dc:creator: Michael Janofsky
item.category: San Francisco Museum of Modern Art
item.media:credit: Anastasiia Sapon for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Geometric Apple Core” by Claes Oldenburg and Coosje van Bruggen is among the items in the exhibition “Reimagined: The Fisher Collection at 10.”

item.title: M.F.A. Boston Celebrates 50 Years of Flowers and Art
item.description: The annual “Art in Bloom” exhibition began in 1976 and has spawned similar events at other museums across the country.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Vora
item.category: Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco
item.media:credit: Neeta Satam for The New York Times
item.media:description: A floral arrangement by Aniyah Donner, inspired by “Bathers With a Turtle” by Henri Matisse, was seen during “Art in Bloom” 2026 at the Saint Louis Art Museum.

item.title: In Indianapolis, a New Contemporary Art Museum Comes With a D.J.
item.description: The 40,000-square-foot space, housed in a former dairy barn, aims to upend expectations of what an art museum can be.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr and Lee Klafczynski
item.media:description: From left, Sylvia Thomas, a poet, writer and musician; the sculptor Bryn Jackson; and the artist India Hines.

item.title: Late Night Takes Sides in the President vs. the Pope
item.description: Jimmy Kimmel said President Trump’s social media post aimed at Pope Leo XIV “is what happens when you sell Bibles instead of reading them.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Oasis, Phil Collins and Sade to Join Rock & Roll Hall of Fame
item.description: Billy Idol, Luther Vandross and Wu-Tang Clan will also be inducted, while New Edition, Mariah Carey and Melissa Etheridge failed to make the final cut.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame and Museum
item.media:credit: Sammy Kogan/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press

item.title: Threats to Library Funding End With Settlement by Trump Administration
item.description: The American Library Association and a union of cultural workers filed a lawsuit arguing that cuts ordered by President Trump were illegal.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.category: Institute of Museum and Library Services
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump had issued an executive order in March 2025 to shutter the Institute of Museum and Library Services, which supports state library agencies.

item.title: Sid Krofft, 96, Dies; Created Zany TV Shows for Children of All Ages
item.description: With his brother Marty, he released a string of wildly inventive programs, including “H.R. Pufnstuf” and “Land of the Lost.” Some became cult favorites, even among adults.
item.dc:creator: Daniel E. Slotnik
item.category: Sigmund and the Sea Monsters (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Alberto E. Rodriguez/Getty Images
item.media:description: Sid Krofft in 2009 accepting an honor at the Seventh Annual TV Land Awards ceremony in Los Angeles. With him was a version of his creation H.R. Pufnstuf.

item.title: Tucker Carlson Is Starting a Publishing Imprint
item.description: Tucker Carlson Books, a joint venture between Carlson’s media company and Skyhorse Publishing, will put out books by Russell Brand, Milo Yiannopoulos and more.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Cheney Orr/Reuters
item.media:description: Tucker Carlson’s new publishing imprint will further expand the influence of its parent company, Skyhorse Publishing, in the conservative publishing space

item.title: Kanye West’s Comeback: Sellouts, a Canceled Festival and Big Questions
item.description: Attempting a comeback after years of antisemitic statements, the artist formerly known as Kanye West is facing consequences while trying to navigate a test of his own making.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica
item.media:credit: Jordan Strauss/Invision, via Associated Press

item.title: At the 2026 Olivier Awards, Musicals Are All the Rage
item.description: Critics reflect on the 2026 Olivier Awards, which recognized homegrown British talent and some productions headed for New York.
item.dc:creator: Houman Barekat, Matt Wolf and Tess Felder
item.media:credit: Jeff Spicer/Getty Images
item.media:description: Rachel Zegler, who won the Olivier Award for best actress in a musical for “Evita,” and Elaine Paige, who debuted that title role in 1978 and received a special award at this year’s Oliviers, at the ceremony in London on Sunday.

item.title: ‘Innocence’ at the Met Opera Transforms With Backstage Magic
item.description: The set of Kaija Saariaho’s opera “Innocence” transforms with astonishing speed. It takes a big, nimble team to make that possible.
item.dc:creator: Darryn King and Vincent Tullo
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times

item.title: A New Focus on Wellness for Students at the School of American Ballet
item.description: At the School of American Ballet’s new health and wellness center, bodies and minds share equal billing and everyone gets the help they need.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and Marcus Maddox
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: ‘DTF St. Louis’ and the Complex Tragedy of the Straight-ish Man
item.description: The relationship between characters played by Jason Bateman and David Harbour went places that most TV shows avoid.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips

item.title: What’s That Musky Aroma at the New Museum?
item.description: The artist Klara Hosnedlova inaugurates OMA’s soaring new atrium stairway at the New Museum.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Leigh Brown
item.media:credit: Sabrina Santiago for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Margo’s Got Money Troubles,’ Plus 5 Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: This adaptation of a novel comes to Apple TV, and the second season of “Beef” premieres on Netflix.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Apple TV
item.media:description: Elle Fanning plays a young mother in “Margo’s Got Money Troubles.”

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Season 3, Episode 4: The Blow-Up
item.description: Valerie is losing her patience and self-control. Who can blame her?
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:credit: Erin Simkin/HBO
item.media:description: Valerie (Lisa Kudrow) is giving her all to A.I.-written slop while Billy (Dan Bucatinsky) is accepting industry honors for doing nothing. She should probably be even angrier than she is.

item.title: ‘Titaníque’ on Broadway: Wild ‘Titanic’ Parody, With Celine Dion as Kooky Guide
item.description: This “Titanic” parody fueled by Dion’s hits, silly ad-libs and pop culture references had the humblest of beginnings. Now the show has docked on Broadway.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Shaping the New Look of ‘Euphoria’
item.description: Natasha Newman-Thomas has stepped in as the new costume designer for the third season of HBO’s chaotic megahit. It’s a lofty task.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
